name: B.A. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan follows a clean CS spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288/CS 331/CS 332 -> CS 350/CS 356 -> CS 435/CS 490 -> CS 491 Senior Project) with prerequisite depths rising smoothly from 0 to 6 and no abrupt difficulty jumps. As a B.A. it deliberately trades depth for breadth, so the upper-level path leans on repeated generic placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') and omits the theory-II and data-science courses that the B.S. requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the repeated generic upper-level elective placeholders with 2-3 named recommended tracks (e.g., applied AI, security, systems) so B.A. students see coherent pathways instead of open slots.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped with a deepest chain of 6 (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> CS 435 -> CS 490 -> CS 491), reasonable for a four-year CS degree, and the core shows no over- or under-constraining. All 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, a lab, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine CS prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or explicit code mappings for YWCC 207, YWCC 307 and PHYS 111A, and resolve the GER 200/GER 300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the term-by-term plan of study and per-course descriptions are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, and it is especially costly for a B.A. whose value proposition (breadth plus a second field) needs an explicit statement.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes describing what a B.A. graduate can design, analyze, and communicate, and how the breadth electives are meant to be used.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The mix transparently spans a CS core, a lighter math/science block (MATH 111/112/333, no required linear algebra), GER, and multiple free electives appropriate to a B.A.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. GER vs. free/CS electives) so the B.A.'s heavier elective share is stated explicitly rather than only implied by the term plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and required courses are legible, but the plan carries parsing artifacts that hurt clarity: a stray cross-program line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science') appears mid-plan in Year 4, a null-code FYS seminar row shows 0 credits, and the COM 312 requirement is split across an 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' fragment row.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study rendering so alternative courses ('COM 312 or COM 313') are single legible entries and remove the cross-program artifact line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science').

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core covers durable, employable fundamentals (databases CS 331, operating systems CS 332, networks CS 356, systems CS 350, Linux tooling CS 288, advanced algorithms CS 435, software engineering CS 490, team Senior Project CS 491), but unlike the B.S. this B.A. requires no data science (CS 301), no cybersecurity (CS 351), and no AI/ML, leaving every modern high-demand area to unspecified electives; CS 288 also still foregrounds a dated PHP/MySQL stack.
dimensions.suggestion: Require at least one modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or security) in the B.A. core and refresh CS 288's stack emphasis away from PHP toward current cloud/data tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course has a present, specific description with real technical substance (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm design/proof and complexity, CS 356 on layered architectures and routing, CS 435 on graphs and hash tables); only the MATH 111/112 entries are terse topic lists rather than full descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better'), and the core chain is internally consistent. Clarity is dinged only by inconsistent phrasing ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher') and prerequisites that point at dangling GER placeholders.

missingTopics.rationale: A 2026 CS degree should give every graduate baseline ML exposure, but this B.A. requires none (CS 301 Introduction to Data Science is required only in the B.S.); add a required or clearly recommended intro-AI/ML course.

The B.A. omits CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity that the B.S. requires, leaving graduates without any guaranteed secure-coding or threat-modeling grounding; make CS 351 a required or default elective.

CS 332 (OS) and CS 356 (networks) cover single-machine and network fundamentals but nothing on cloud deployment, containers, or distributed computing that dominate current practice; add a cloud/distributed-systems elective.

The B.A. requires only MATH 333, omitting the MATH 337 Linear Algebra the B.S. requires, which is foundational for graphics, ML, and data work; recommend or require linear algebra for students eyeing those tracks.

CS 490 is the sole SWE course and CS 288 still centers PHP/MySQL, so version control, testing/CI-CD, and API design are underweighted; modernize CS 288 or add a practical software-engineering elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: Employers now expect graduates to build with and reason about LLM tooling; CS 490 and the CS 491 Senior Project are natural places to require an AI-assisted development component.
Data and model pipelines are among the fastest-growing CS roles, yet this B.A. has no required data course; a data-engineering elective would let breadth-oriented B.A. students target this market.
Secure defaults and privacy-by-design are becoming baseline professional expectations, reinforcing the case for embedding CS 351 content rather than leaving security optional.
The existing IS 350 (Computers, Society and Ethics) is a strong hook to extend into algorithmic accountability and AI governance, differentiating the B.A.'s breadth angle.
Industry has shifted to containerized, serverless deployment; positioning an elective here would keep the B.A. current without adding heavy theory load.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes for the B.A., which are entirely absent from the catalog data.
Add at least one required modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or cybersecurity) so the B.A. does not leave every high-demand area to unspecified electives.
Replace the repeated generic elective placeholders with named recommended tracks (applied AI, security, systems) to give the upper-level path coherence.
Modernize CS 288's dated PHP/MySQL emphasis toward current cloud and data tooling.
Clean plan-of-study parsing artifacts (the stray '228 B.S. in Computer Science' line and split 'or COM 313' rows) and resolve the 5 dangling references (YWCC 207/307, PHYS 111A, GER 200/300).
